explanation: -(으)ㄹ 걸(요) is used to express an unconfirmed assumption, meaning 'It probably is...' or 'I bet...'. It is a casual way to state your guess to a friend.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 걸(요)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 걸요 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 그 식당은 너무 비쌀 걸요? — I bet that restaurant is too expensive.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 걸(요)
rules.description: Attach 을 걸요 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 사이즈가 좀 작을 걸요 — The size will probably be a bit small.

rules.title: Past Tense → + 았/었을 걸(요)
rules.description: You can attach this to the past tense to guess about a past event.

examples.english: 지금쯤 도착했을 걸요? — They probably arrived by now.

notes.title: Not for facts
notes.text: This grammar strictly shows a guess. If you actually know for sure, use -(으)ㄹ 거예요 instead.

notes.title: Intonation matters
notes.text: The sentence often ends with a slight rising intonation, like you are tossing your opinion out for consideration.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 테니(까) has two different uses. The first use is to volunteer for a task while asking the listener to do something else. It translates to 'I will do X, so you do Y.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 테니까
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 테니까 to vowel stems. The subject must be 'I'.

examples.english: 제가 먼저 갈 테니까 천천히 오세요 — I will go first, so please come slowly.
제가 청소할 테니까 쉬세요 — I will clean, so you rest.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 테니까
rules.description: Attach 을 테니까 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 제가 식당을 찾을 테니까 걱정하지 마세요 — I will find a restaurant, so don't worry.

notes.title: Commands and suggestions
notes.text: The second half of the sentence is almost always a command (-(으)세요) or a suggestion (-(으)ㅂ시다).

notes.title: First person only
notes.text: For this specific 'intention' meaning, the person doing the action before 테니까 MUST be you.

explanation: -고 보면 means 'If you do [Verb] and then reflect on it' or 'If you look at it after doing'. Use it to express a realization or a truth you discover only after a continuous action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 고 보면
rules.description: Attach 고 보면 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 알고 보면 좋은 사람이에요 — If you get to know him (and look closely), he is a good person.
살고 보면 다 익숙해져요 — As you live (and experience it), you get used to everything.

notes.title: vs -고 보니(까)
notes.text: -고 보면 is for general truths or future conditional realizations (If you do this, you will realize). -고 보니까 is for a specific past realization (I did this, and I realized).

explanation: -고 보니(까) means 'Having done [Verb], I realized that...'. Use it to share a sudden discovery or realization that occurred directly after completing an action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 고 보니까
rules.description: Attach 고 보니까 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 먹고 보니까 유통기한이 지났어요 — Having eaten it, I realized it was expired.
그 말을 듣고 보니 네 말이 맞네 — Having heard that, I realize you are right.

notes.title: Often shortened
notes.text: In conversation, the '까' is frequently dropped, leaving just -고 보니.

notes.title: No past tense before it
notes.text: Do not attach the past tense marker (-았/었-) before 고 보니까. Even though the action is in the past, it is attached to the dictionary stem.

explanation: -다가 indicates an interruption. It means 'while doing A, I stopped and shifted to doing B'. The action in the first half is cut short or transitions into the second half.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 다가
rules.description: Attach 다가 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 학교에 가다가 친구를 만났어요 — While going to school (I stopped/transitioned), I met a friend.
공부하다가 잠이 들었어요 — While studying, I fell asleep.

notes.title: Same subject
notes.text: The subject of the first action must be the exact same as the subject of the second action.

notes.title: vs -(으)면서
notes.text: -(으)면서 means doing two things at the exact same time (eating while watching TV). -다가 means shifting from one to another (watching TV, then stopping to eat).

explanation: -다가(는) acts as a warning. It translates to 'If you keep doing[Verb], a negative result will happen'. The 는 adds emphasis to the warning.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 다가는
rules.description: Attach 다가는 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 계속 놀다가는 시험에 떨어질 거예요 — If you keep playing around, you will fail the test.
이렇게 많이 먹다가는 배탈이 날 거예요 — If you keep eating this much, you'll get a stomachache.

notes.title: Always negative
notes.text: The second half of the sentence using -다가는 will always contain a negative consequence or a bad prediction.

explanation: -(ㄴ/는)다면 is the hypothetical 'if'. While -(으)면 is for normal conditions (If it rains...), this grammar is used for unlikely, dramatic, or entirely imaginary scenarios.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + ㄴ/는다면
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다면 to vowel stems and 는다면 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 다시 태어난다면 새가 되고 싶어요 — If I were born again, I'd want to be a bird.

rules.title: Adjectives & Past Tense → + 다면
rules.description: Attach 다면 directly to adjectives or the past tense marker.

examples.english: 그게 너무 비싸다면 안 살게요 — If (it turns out that) it's too expensive, I won't buy it.
어제 거기에 갔다면 그를 만났을 거예요 — If you had gone there yesterday, you would have met him.

notes.title: With nouns
notes.text: For nouns, use (이)라면. (e.g., 내가 부자라면... = If I were rich...).

explanation: N-(이)라도 means 'at least [Noun]' or 'even [Noun]'. Use it to suggest a second-best option when your first choice isn't available, showing that it will suffice.

rules.title: Noun ending in a vowel → + 라도
rules.description: Attach 라도 to nouns without a final consonant.

examples.english: 밥이 없으면 커피라도 마실래요? — If there's no food, do you want to at least drink coffee?

rules.title: Noun ending in a consonant → + 이라도
rules.description: Attach 이라도 to nouns with a final consonant.

examples.english: 라면이라도 먹자 — Let's at least eat ramen (since there's nothing else).

notes.title: With question words
notes.text: When combined with question words, it means 'any'. 누구라도 (anyone), 언제라도 (anytime), 어디라도 (anywhere).

explanation: -뿐(만 아니라) means 'Not only A, but also B'. It shows that the situation includes the first item and extends to an additional item.

rules.title: Noun + 뿐만 아니라
rules.description: Attach 뿐만 아니라 directly to nouns.

examples.english: 한국어뿐만 아니라 영어도 잘해요 — He speaks not only Korean, but also English well.

rules.title: Verb + (으)ㄹ 뿐만 아니라
rules.description: Attach ㄹ/을 뿐만 아니라 to verb stems.

examples.english: 그녀는 똑똑할 뿐만 아니라 친절해요 — She is not only smart, but also kind.

notes.title: Using 뿐이다 alone
notes.text: If you drop '만 아니라' and just use 'N-뿐이다', it means 'There is ONLY [Noun]'. (e.g., 나에겐 너뿐이야 = I only have you).

explanation: -조차 and -마저 both translate to 'even', but they are used in extreme or negative situations. They emphasize that out of all possibilities, even the most basic or unexpected thing is included.

rules.title: Noun + 조차
rules.description: Often used with a negative ending to mean 'not even the most basic thing'.

examples.english: 너무 당황해서 이름조차 기억나지 않았어요 — I was so panicked I couldn't even remember my name.

rules.title: Noun + 마저
rules.description: Often implies that the LAST remaining thing has also crossed over to a negative state.

examples.english: 너마저 나를 떠나는구나 — Even you (the last person I trusted) are leaving me.

notes.title: vs -도
notes.text: -도 is the normal 'also/even'. -조차 and -마저 are dramatic and carry heavy emotional weight, usually sadness, shock, or disappointment.

explanation: -대신(에) means 'instead of'. You can use it to swap one noun for another, or to compensate for an action.

rules.title: Noun + 대신(에)
rules.description: Attach 대신(에) directly to a noun.

examples.english: 커피 대신에 차를 마실게요 — I will drink tea instead of coffee.

rules.title: Verb + 는 대신(에)
rules.description: Attach 는 대신(에) to an action verb.

examples.english: 제가 도와주는 대신에 밥을 사 주세요 — Instead of (in exchange for) me helping you, buy me a meal.

notes.title: Dropping 에
notes.text: The '에' is completely optional and frequently dropped in spoken Korean. '커피 대신' sounds perfectly natural.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 데(에) 좋다/나쁘다 means 'to be good/bad for doing [Verb]'. Here, '데' means 'situation' or 'the case of'.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + 는 데 좋다
rules.description: Attach 는 데 좋다/나쁘다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 이 차는 잠을 자는 데에 좋아요 — This tea is good for sleeping.
드라마는 한국어 배우는 데 도움이 돼요 — Dramas are helpful for learning Korean.

notes.title: Spacing is crucial!
notes.text: You MUST put a space before 데 (는 데). If you write it together (-는데), it becomes the grammar point for 'but/and' (ID 79).

notes.title: Other adjectives
notes.text: You can use this with other adjectives like 필요하다 (This is necessary for...) or 쓰다 (I spent money on/for...).

explanation: -는 법이다 translates to 'It is a rule that...' or 'It is natural that...'. Use it to state a universal truth, a law of nature, or a proverb.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 는 법이다
rules.description: Attach 는 법이다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 시간이 지나면 잊혀지는 법이에요 — It is natural to be forgotten as time passes.
물가는 항상 오르는 법이에요 — It is a rule (fact of life) that prices always go up.

notes.title: Literal meaning
notes.text: 법 literally means 'law' or 'rule'. So you are saying 'It is the law of the universe that...'

explanation: -는 척하다 means 'to pretend to'. Use it when someone is faking an action or a state to deceive others.

rules.title: Present Verb → + 는 척하다
rules.description: Attach 는 척하다 to an action verb happening right now.

examples.english: 아기가 자는 척해요 — The baby is pretending to sleep.

rules.title: Adjectives & Past Verbs → + (으)ㄴ 척하다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 척하다 to adjectives or past completed actions.

examples.english: 모를면서 아는 척하지 마세요 — Don't pretend to know when you don't.
친한 척했어요 — We pretended to be close.

notes.title: Interchangeable with 체하다
notes.text: You will sometimes see -는 체하다 in books. It means the exact same thing as -는 척하다, but 척하다 is much more common in speaking.

exceptions.name: ㄹ drop
exceptions.description: Verbs ending in ㄹ drop it before adding ㄴ 척하다.
exceptions.example: 알다 (to know) → 아는 척하다 (pretend to know).

explanation: -는 통에 and -(으)ㄴ 탓에 both mean 'because of [X]', but they exclusively carry a negative, blaming nuance. -는 통에 specifically implies a noisy, chaotic, or distracting situation.

rules.title: Action Verb + 는 통에
rules.description: Use for a chaotic, distracting action causing a bad result.

examples.english: 사람들이 떠드는 통에 집중할 수 없었어요 — Because people were chatting loudly, I couldn't focus.

rules.title: Adjectives/Verbs + (으)ㄴ/는 탓에
rules.description: Use 탓에 to directly point the finger of blame at a cause.

examples.english: 비가 오는 탓에 행사가 취소됐어요 — The event was canceled due to the rain.

notes.title: Never use for good results
notes.text: Do not say 'I passed the test 탓에/통에'. Blame words are only for bad outcomes.

notes.title: With Nouns
notes.text: You can attach 탓에 directly to nouns. (e.g., 날씨 탓에 = due to the weather).

explanation: -는 덕분에 is the exact opposite of 탓에. It means 'thanks to' and is exclusively used when a positive cause brings about a positive, beneficial result.

rules.title: Past Verbs → + (으)ㄴ 덕분에
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 덕분에 to a past helpful action.

examples.english: 도와주신 덕분에 빨리 끝났어요 — Thanks to you helping me, it finished quickly.

rules.title: Nouns → + 덕분에
rules.description: Attach 덕분에 directly to nouns.

examples.english: 선생님 덕분에 합격했어요 — Thanks to the teacher, I passed.

notes.title: Not for negative results
notes.text: It sounds very sarcastic if you use this for a bad outcome. Don't say 'Thanks to the rain, we couldn't go'.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 걸 보니(까) means 'Judging from the fact that...'. Use it when you observe a piece of visual evidence and draw a logical conclusion from it.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + 는 걸 보니까
rules.description: Attach 는 걸 보니까 to an action you are observing.

examples.english: 웃는 걸 보니까 기분이 좋은가 봐요 — Judging from the fact that he's smiling, he seems to be in a good mood.

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ 걸 보니까
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 걸 보니까 to state an observed fact.

examples.english: 사람이 많은 걸 보니까 맛집인가 봐요 — Judging from the fact that there are many people, it must be a good restaurant.

notes.title: Natural pairings
notes.text: The second half of the sentence is almost always a guess, so it pairs perfectly with -나 보다, -(으)ㄹ 것 같다, or -(으)ㄹ 텐데.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 채(로) means 'while in the state of having done [X]'. It is used to describe doing a second action while maintaining the physical state or result of the first action.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 채로
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 채로 to vowel stems.

examples.english: TV를 켠 채로 잠이 들었어요 — I fell asleep with the TV (left) on.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 채로
rules.description: Attach 은 채로 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 신발을 신은 채로 방에 들어갔어요 — I went into the room with my shoes on.

notes.title: vs -(으)면서
notes.text: -(으)면서 is for two active motions happening at once (eating while walking). -(으)ㄴ 채로 is for a frozen state (eyes closed, glasses on, door open) while doing something else.

explanation: -곤 하다 expresses a habit. It means 'I often do...' or 'I tend to...'. It shows that an action is repeated frequently, but not necessarily on a strict schedule.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 곤 하다
rules.description: Attach 곤 하다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 주말에는 혼자 영화를 볼러 가곤 해요 — On weekends, I often go to watch movies alone.

rules.title: Past Tense → + 곤 했다
rules.description: Use the past tense to talk about a habit you used to have, meaning 'used to'.

examples.english: 어렸을 때 이 공원에서 놀곤 했어요 — When I was young, I used to play in this park.

notes.title: Origin
notes.text: -곤 is a contraction of -고는. It literally means 'doing this and then...' implying a recurring sequence.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 만하다 means 'worth doing' or 'bearable'. Use it to recommend something because it has enough value to justify doing it, or to say a bad situation isn't entirely terrible.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 만하다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 만하다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 제주도는 한 번쯤 갈 만해요 — Jeju Island is worth going to at least once.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 만하다
rules.description: Attach 을 만하다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 이 식당 음식은 먹을 만해요 — This restaurant's food is bearable (decent enough to eat).

notes.title: Nuance check
notes.text: Depending on your tone, 먹을 만해요 can mean 'It's a highly recommended dish' OR 'It's not amazing, but it's okay enough to eat'.

explanation: -다시피 means 'as you [know/see/hear]'. It confirms that the listener is already aware of the fact you are about to state.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 다시피
rules.description: Attach 다시피 to verbs related to perception (know, see, hear, feel).

examples.english: 아시다시피, 저는 외국인이에요 — As you know, I am a foreigner.
보시다시피, 비가 많이 오고 있어요 — As you can see, it is raining a lot.

notes.title: Honorifics are standard
notes.text: Because you are usually addressing someone else politely, it is almost always combined with the honorific -(으)시. (알다 -> 아시다시피, 보다 -> 보시다시피).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ 셈이다 means 'It is practically the same as' or 'It amounts to'. You use this when a situation isn't 100% exactly X, but mathematically or logically, it is so close that you can consider it X.

rules.title: Adjectives & Past Verbs → + (으)ㄴ 셈이다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 셈이다 to past actions.

examples.english: 일주일에 5번 봤으니까 매일 만난 셈이에요 — Since we saw each other 5 times a week, it's practically like we met every day.

rules.title: Present Verbs → + 는 셈이다
rules.description: Attach 는 셈이다 to present facts.

examples.english: 돈을 안 썼으니까 돈을 버는 셈이에요 — Since I didn't spend money, it's basically the same as earning money.

notes.title: Similar to
notes.text: This is very similar to -와/과 마찬가지다 (It is the same as), but 셈 focuses more on the calculation or logical conclusion.

explanation: -나 보다 / -(으)ㄴ가 보다 means 'It seems like' or 'I guess'. While -(으)ㄹ 것 같다 is just a gut feeling, you use this grammar when you have observed a CLUE or piece of evidence that makes you assume something.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + 나 보다
rules.description: Attach 나 보다 to action verbs.

examples.english: 우산이 있는 걸 보니, 비가 오나 봐요 — Seeing that people have umbrellas, I guess it's raining.

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ가 보다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ가 보다 to adjectives.

examples.english: 약국에 가는 걸 보니, 아픈가 봐요 — Seeing him go to the pharmacy, he must be sick.

rules.title: Past Tense → + 았/었나 보다
rules.description: Always use 나 보다 after the past tense marker.

examples.english: 불이 꺼진 걸 보니, 끝났나 봐요 — Seeing the lights are off, I guess it ended.

notes.title: Not for personal plans
notes.text: You cannot use this to guess about your own actions. You can't say 'I guess I am eating' (내가 먹나 봐요).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ 듯하다 means 'It seems as if...'. It is a slightly more formal, literary, or written equivalent of -(으)ㄴ/는 것 같다.

rules.title: Modifiers + 듯하다
rules.description: Use standard noun modifier rules (는, (으)ㄴ, (으)ㄹ) and attach 듯하다.

examples.english: 내일은 비가 올 듯합니다 — It seems as if it will rain tomorrow.
요즘 많이 바쁜 듯해요 — It appears that you are very busy lately.

notes.title: Spoken contraction
notes.text: In conversation, 듯하다 is often shortened to just -는 듯. (e.g., 비가 올 듯 = looks like rain).

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 리가 없다 means 'There is no way that...' or 'It's impossible that...'. Use it to strongly deny a possibility based on your logic or disbelief.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 리가 없다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 그 사람이 그런 말을 할 리가 없어요 — There is no way that person would say such a thing.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach 을 리가 없다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 사이즈가 작을 리가 없어요. 제일 큰 옷이에요 — It's impossible for it to be small. It's the biggest size.

notes.title: Question form
notes.text: You can turn it into a rhetorical question: 그럴 리가요? (How could that be? / No way!).

explanation: The second use of -(으)ㄹ 테니(까) is to express a strong assumption. It means 'I expect/assume it will be X, so you should do Y'. Use it to give advice based on your logical guess.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 테니까
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 테니까 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 지금 바쁠 테니까 나중에 전화하세요 — I assume he is busy right now, so call later.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 테니까
rules.description: Attach 을 테니까 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 퇴근 시간이라 길이 막힐 테니까 지하철을 타세요 — I expect traffic will be bad since it's rush hour, so take the subway.

notes.title: vs. First-person use
notes.text: Remember ID 161! If the subject is 'I', it means 'I WILL do this'. If the subject is anything else (weather, a third person, traffic), it means 'I ASSUME it will be this'.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 텐데(요) means 'I expect/guess that... but/so...'. It is used to show a strong assumption while introducing a related concern, suggestion, or contrasting background information.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 텐데
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 텐데 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 많이 피곤하실 텐데 일찍 쉬세요 — You must be very tired, so please rest early.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 텐데
rules.description: Attach 을 텐데 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 지금 가면 늦을 텐데요... — I expect you'll be late if you go now (trailing off with concern).

notes.title: Trailing off
notes.text: It is extremely common to end sentences with -(으)ㄹ 텐데요. It leaves the sentence open, expressing worry or hesitation without directly stating the bad news.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 걸 그랬다 is the standard way to express regret. It translates to 'I should have done [X]'. It literally implies 'It would have been better if I had done it'.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 걸 그랬다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 걸 그랬다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 세일할 때 살 걸 그랬어요 — I should have bought it when it was on sale.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 걸 그랬다
rules.description: Attach 을 걸 그랬다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 밥을 더 먹을 걸 그랬어요 — I should have eaten more food.

notes.title: Expressing 'I shouldn't have'
notes.text: To regret something you DID do, use the negative -지 말 걸 그랬다. (e.g., 말하지 말 걸 그랬어요 = I shouldn't have said it).

explanation: -기 마련이다 means 'It is bound to happen' or 'It is natural that'. It is used to state inevitable outcomes or general truths of human nature and the world.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 마련이다
rules.description: Attach 기 마련이다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 사람은 누구나 실수하기 마련이에요 — Everyone is bound to make mistakes.
시간이 흐를면 마음도 변하기 마련이죠 — As time passes, it's natural for feelings to change.

notes.title: Similar to
notes.text: It has almost the exact same meaning and usage as -는 법이다 (ID 172). Both point out universal truths.

explanation: -기 십상이다 means 'Very likely to' or 'It is easy to [make a mistake]'. It is almost always used to warn about a negative probability or an easy trap to fall into.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 십상이다
rules.description: Attach 기 십상이다 directly to the verb stem.

examples.english: 얇게 입으면 감기에 걸리기 십상이에요 — If you dress lightly, you are very likely to catch a cold.
메모를 안 하면 잊어버리기 십상이에요 — If you don't take notes, it's easy to forget.

notes.title: Etymology
notes.text: 십상 comes from the Hanja phrase '십중팔구' (Eight or nine out of ten). So it literally means a 80-90% chance of a bad thing happening.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다 means 'almost did' or 'nearly happened'. You use this when a drastic event almost took place, but you narrowly escaped it. It is always used in the past tense.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 뻔했다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 뻔했다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 사고가 날 뻔했어요 — I almost got into an accident.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 뻔했다
rules.description: Attach 을 뻔했다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 너무 매워서 죽을 뻔했어요 — It was so spicy I almost died.

notes.title: Always past tense
notes.text: Because the danger has already been avoided by the time you speak, it must always end in 뻔했다, never 뻔하다.

notes.title: Adding 하마터면
notes.text: You will often hear the adverb 하마터면 (by a hair's breadth) at the start of the sentence for dramatic effect. 하마터면 늦을 뻔했어요! (I was THIS close to being late!).
